content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 8,
1993
Giovanna Stark
Executive Director
Child Development Programs
Advisory Committee
915 Capitol Mall,
Room 336
Sacramento,
CA 95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93-058
Dear Ms.
Stark:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding
whether the Child Development Programs Advisory Committee
(CDPAC)
is an “agency” subject to the provisions of the Political Reform
Act
(“the Act”.)
1
QUESTION
You have asked if CDPAC has or should have a conflict of
interest code.
CONCLUS ION
A review of records indicates that CDPAC has not adopted a
conflict of interest code.
CDPAC must adopt a conflict of
interest code because its members or employees make or participate
in the making of governmental decisions.
FACTS
CDPAC is a citizens review and advisory board comprised of 22
appointed citizens and representatives of state departments with a
very small staff of which you are the Executive Director.
CDPAC
was established in 1965 by AB 1331 and since then has been
responsible for reviewing and evaluating the effectiveness of
child development programs and the need for services to children.
The advisory committee conducts monthly meetings which are open to
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
g.
All references to rgu1ations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
A—93 —058
Page
2
the public.
It is at these meetings that
CDPAC makes its
recommendations which are published in the minutes.
It
additionally promulgates its recommendations in special reports.
It is our understanding that CDPAC’s role and mandate is to advise
and assist the Department of Education on implementation of the
State Plan,
and to review the effectiveness of child care and
development programs and the need for children’s services.
Specifically CDPAC acts to:
(a)
Advise and assist the Superintendent of Public
Instruction, the Secretary of Child Development and
Education and the Secretary of Health and Welfare with
the federal child care and development block grant
funds.
(b)
Facilitate and coordinate local and State planning on
children’ s issues.
(c)
Identify successful models throughout the state related
to both child care planning and programs,
such as school
age child care.
(d)
Review work related programs such as
GAIN and TCC and
develop new strategies to bettermeet the child care
needs of working families.
Ce)
Develop and distribute information on employer supportei.
child care.
(f)
Address issues as they arise in the public forum,
related to special needs,
licensing, registration,
parental choice,
consumer education,
local planning and
data collection procedures.
(g)
Maintain an active clearinghouse on information that
relates to young children.
On February 24,
1993,
you indicated in a telephone
conversation that you are in the process of approving a contract
to either conduct research or produce a report.
ANALYSIS
Government Code Section 82019 defines a designated employee
as any officer,
employee,
member or consultant of any agency whose
position with the agency is designated in a conflict of interest
code because the position entails the making or participation in
the making of decisions which may foreseeably affect any financial
interest.
Unsalaried members of boards or commissions which serve
a solely advisory function are excluded from the definition.
A board or coimniss ion possesses decision—making authority
whenever it makes substantive recommendations which are,
and over
an extended period of time have been, regularly approved without
significant amendment or modification by another public official


Advice File No.
A—93 —058
Page
3
or governmental agency.
(Regulation 18700(a) (1) (C),
copy
enclosed.)
You have indicated that your committee’s recommendations are
made in the course of your open monthly meetings and also in
special reports.
You have also indicated your recommendations are
not binding on any policy making body and you can only hope that
policy makers will look at them.
However,
Commission Regulation 18700(b)
indicates that “a
public official
“makes a governmental decision”
when he or she,
acting within the authority of his or her office:
(1)
Votes on a matter;
(2)
Appoints a person;
(3)
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of
action;
(4)
Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his
or her agency;... (Emphasis added.)
Since CDPAC does have the authority to enter into contractual
agreements, its employees are making governmental decisions.
Therefore,
CDPAC must adopt a conflict of interest code.
If the
executive director alone has the authority to enter into a
contractual agreement on behalf of the agency,
then only the
executive director would be designated to complete conflict of
interest statements.
However, if the advisory committee or any
other employees of CDPAC may influence the process of contracting
for goods or services on behalf of the agency they will also be
required to be designated in the conflict of interest code for the
agency.
If you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Cou sel
.J
By:
kyne P.
Imberi
Political Reform Consultant

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked if CDPAC has or should have a conflict of
interest code.

CONCLUSION: A review of records indicates that CDPAC has not adopted a
conflict of interest code.
CDPAC must adopt a conflict of
interest code because its members or employees make or participate
in the making of governmental decisions.
